answer:
\boxed{D}

An enlarged kidney (e.g., hydronephrosis or polycystic kidney disease) typically produces a **dull percussion note** due to its solid or fluid-filled nature, unlike the tympanic sound of air-filled structures. The other options are either too absolute (e.g., "always" in benign tumors, aneurysms, or gallstones) or incorrect (e.g., hernias are not always movable, ovarian cysts don’t disappear with catheterization, or stomach ulcers don’t cause mass disappearance post-meal).
